coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and selective electrochemical aptasensor with gold-aspartic acid, glycine acid-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot nanohybrid for detection of α-amanitin in blood
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Qiyue
Li, Ruiyi
Sun, Xiulan
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Poisonous mushroom may cause fatal harm to human and animal, but its rapid detection still faces a great challenge. The paper reports synthesis of gold-aspartic acid, glycine acid-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot nanohybrid (AGB-GQD@Au) for the electrochemical detection of α-amanitin. AGB-GQD was prepared by pyrolysis and then reacted with chloroauric acid to produce gold nanoparticles. AGB-GQD@Au offers 12.5 nm-sized particles and Schottky heterojunction, improving the catalytic activity. AGB-GQD@Au connected with hairpin DNA and thionine by Au–S bonds was used as redox probe for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin coupled with one target-induced DNA cycle amplification strategy. α-Amanitin specifically hybridizes with aptamer in duplex DNA to release auxiliary strand DNA. The released DNA triggers one DNA cycle process and brings one redox probe to the electrode surface. By the DNA cycle, one target brings many redox probes to the electrode surface, producing a significant signal amplification. The detection signal was further enhanced by the catalysis of AGB-GQD@Au towards redox of thionine. Differential pulse voltammetric current increases linearly with the increasing α-amanitin in the range from 4 to 4 × 105 fM with the detection limit of 1.2 fΜ (S/N = 3). The analytical method provides advantages of sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability. It has been successfully applied in electrochemical detection of α-amanitin in blood.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold-graphene composite
Schottky heterojunction catalyst
Aptasensor
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910203) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFA0910203 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115 ). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A), TEM image (B), XRD pattern (C) and FTIR spectrum (D) of AGB-GQD@Au
Total (A), C1s (B), B1s (C), Au 4f (D), N1s (E) and O1s (F) XPS spectra of AGB-GQD@Au
Scheme for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin.

CV curves and EIS plots in a 5.0 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] in the PBS of pH 7 of the bare GE (a), H1/GE (b), MCH/H1/GE incubated in a mixed AP/AS duplex DNA solution with H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes in the absence (c) and the presence of 4 × 10−12 M α-amanitin (d).
DPV curves of the proposed sensing platform in a PBS of pH 7 in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 4 × 10−12 M α-amanitin.

DPV curves (A) in a PBS of pH 7 in the presence of 4, 20, 40, 200, 400, 2000, 4000, 20000, 40000, 40000, 200000 and 400000 fM α-amanitin (from bottom to top) and the relationship curve (B) of DPV peak current (Ip) with the logarithm value of α-amanitin concentration.
The results for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin in blood samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and selective electrochemical aptasensor with gold-aspartic acid, glycine acid-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot nanohybrid for detection of α-amanitin in blood

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poisonous mushroom may cause fatal harm to human and animal, but its rapid detection still faces a great challenge. The paper reports synthesis of gold-aspartic acid, glycine acid-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot nanohybrid (AGB-GQD@Au) for the electrochemical detection of α-amanitin. AGB-GQD was prepared by pyrolysis and then reacted with chloroauric acid to produce gold nanoparticles. AGB-GQD@Au offers 12.5 nm-sized particles and Schottky heterojunction, improving the catalytic activity. AGB-GQD@Au connected with hairpin DNA and thionine by Au–S bonds was used as redox probe for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin coupled with one target-induced DNA cycle amplification strategy. α-Amanitin specifically hybridizes with aptamer in duplex DNA to release auxiliary strand DNA. The released DNA triggers one DNA cycle process and brings one redox probe to the electrode surface. By the DNA cycle, one target brings many redox probes to the electrode surface, producing a significant signal amplification. The detection signal was further enhanced by the catalysis of AGB-GQD@Au towards redox of thionine. Differential pulse voltammetric current increases linearly with the increasing α-amanitin in the range from 4 to 4 × 105 fM with the detection limit of 1.2 fΜ (S/N = 3). The analytical method provides advantages of sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability. It has been successfully applied in electrochemical detection of α-amanitin in blood.

ce:section.ce:para: Mushrooms become favorite ingredients due to their delicious taste. However, poisonous mushrooms may cause fatal harm to humans and animals [1]. In poisonous mushrooms, α-amanitin is the most common toxin and is often involved in human and animal intoxication and death [2]. α-Amanitin inhibits the transcription by binding to RNA polymerase II [3] and causes early morphological and functional alterations in hepatocytes [4]. The patients suffering from α-amanitin poisoning die from acute liver failure in 2–8 days if not properly diagnosed and treated in time. Thus, development of a reliable method for rapid detection of α-amanitin in blood is important to poisoned patient's therapy [5]. Many techniques have been developed for the detection of α-amanitin, including liquid chromatography-triple quadrupole tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [6], RNA polymerase inhibition method and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7], and lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) [8]. Among these methods, LC-MS/MS offers high sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability for the detection of α-amanitin. However, it needs complex sample pretreatments, expensive equipment and skilled technician. ELISA offers high sensitivity and selectivity, but it requires the use of high-purity antibody and enzyme. However, the preparation of high-quality antibody is complex, time-consuming and expensive. To overcome these problems, aptamer has been designed and used as a substitute for antibody for the detection of α-amanitin [9]. To date, aptemers have been widely applied in the preparation of biosensors for various targets [10]. Electrochemical aptasensor received a more attention because of its high sensitivity, selectivity, rapidity and low cost [11]. However, there is no electrochemical aptasensor reported for the detection of α-amanitin.

Some noble metal nanocrystals have been synthesized and studied for construction of electrochemical aptasensors, such as Au [12], Ag [13], Pt [14], Pd [15] and Rh [16]. Because of high catalytic activity and electrical conductivity, Au nanocrystal is the most commonly used sensing material in electrochemical aptasensors [17,18]. Two ways were devoted to fabricating Au nanocrystal to improve the electrochemical properties. One way is to increase the exposure of high-index faces of Au nanocrystal. As a high-index face offers a better catalytic activity than a low-index face, the Au nanocrystal with more exposed high-index faces always exhibits an improved catalytic activity [19]. Our group synthesized a gold nanocrystal by reduction of HAuCl4 with ascorbic acid in the presence of CTAC, KBr and KI [20]. The resultant Au nanocrystal shows trioctahedral-like nanostructure with fully exposed high-index facets. Such a unique structure achieves high chemical activity and catalytic activity. Another way is to introduce graphene to make metal-graphene composite [21–25]. The resultant composite can offer a much higher electron/electrolyte conductivity compared with single Au and single graphene because of the formation of three-dimensional graphene framework. However, hydrophilic electrolyte is difficult to access the electrode surface with the composite during electrochemical detection because of its high hydrophobicity. To solve the problem, researchers used functional graphene quantum dot instead of common graphene to make metal-graphene composite [26–28]. The result shows that the introduction of graphene quantum dots improves water solubility and catalytic activity. Although progress was made, the construction of an electrochemical aptasensor with Au-graphene nanohybrid for the detection of α-amanitin in blood with an ideal analytical performance still faces a great challenge.

In the study, we reported synthesis of gold-aspartic acid, glycine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot nanohybrid (AGB-GQD@Au). The as-synthesized AGB-GQD@Au exhibits small particle size of gold nanocrystals and high catalytic activity. The electrochemical aptasensor based on AGB-GQD@Au coupled with DNA cycle signal amplification provides advantage of high sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability for the detection of α-amanitin in blood.

Citric acid, aspartic acid (Asp), glycine acid (Gly), boric acid (H3BO3), gold chloroauric (HAuCl4), tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydro-chloride (TCEP) and K4[Fe(CN)6] were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. α-Amanitin was obtained from Enzo Life Sciences (Farmingdale, New York). All oligonucleotides were made and purified by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The concentration of oligonucleotides was determined by measuring absorption at 260 nm. The DNA stock solution was prepared in the Tris/Mg/K buffer containing 20 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM MgCl2, 1 mM CaCl2 and 5 mM KCl at pH 7.4 and stored at −20 °C for further use. Aptamer (AP, 5′-GACATATTCAGTCTGACAGCGGA AGCGGGTCAGTCCAACTCACGGTCTCGGATGCACGGGAGATGGACGAATATCGTCTAGC-3′), auxiliary strand DNA (AS, 5′-GAC CGTGAGTTGGACTGACCCGC-3′), hairpin DNA 1 (H1, 5′–SH–(CH2)6-GCGGGTCAGTCCAACGCGTGTAATGGACTGAAAT-3′), and hairpin DNA 2 (H2, 5′-ATTTCAGTCCA TTACACGCGTTGGACTG ACCCGC–(CH2)6–SH-3′). Phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (Na2HPO4–NaH2PO4–KCl, 0.1 M, pH 7.0) and (1 mM) was prepared in the laboratory. Other materials and reagents used were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical reagent Co., Ltd. The deionized (DI) water was produced using a Millipore Milli-Q grade with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements were conducted on HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were conducted on JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the Japanese island ferry + Axis Supra + electron spectrometer. Infrared spectrum (IR) was measured on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer.

Citric acid was mixed with aspartic acid, glycine acid and boric acid with a molar ratio of 1:1:0.2:0.2 and then heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The resultant AGB-GQD crude product was dissolved in water to form 200 mg mL−1 of AGB-GQD solution. Followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 2 min to remove large graphene sheet, dialysis to remove unreacted raw material and finally freeze-drying to obtain AGB-GQD solid product.

The AGB-GQD solution (50 mL, 2 mg mL−1) was heated to boiling, added 3 mL of the HAuCl4 solution (10 mM) under stirring, keep the temperature for 1 min and then cooled to room temperature. Followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, washing for 3 times with 5 mL water and centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The collected AGB-GQD@Au was again dispersed in water to form 2 mg mL−1 of AGB-GQD@Au dispersion.

The H2 solution was heated at 95 °C for 5 min and cooled to ambient temperature to form a hairpin structure. The pre-treated H2 solution (200 μL, 10 μM) was mixed with an equal volume of tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP) solution (1 mM). It was orderly treated by incubation at ambient temperature for 1 h, addition of 600 μL of AGB-GQD@Au dispersion, incubation for 24 h, and centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. To obtain H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI redox probe, the collected H2-AGB-GQD@Au was mixed with 200 μL of thionine (THI) solution (0.1 mM). Followed by incubation at ambient temperature for 12 h and centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The collected H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI redox probes were dispersed in 1 mL of Tri-EDTA buffer of pH 7.4 (TE buffer).

The H1 solution was heated at 95 °C for 5 min and cooled to ambient temperature to form a hairpin structure. The pre-treated H1 solution (5 μL, 5 μM) was mixed with 20 μL of the TCEP solution (1 mM) and then incubated at ambient temperature for 30 min. After 5 μL of the above solution was dropped on the surface of the gold electrode (GE), the sensor was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, washed with water and dried in N2 current in sequence. Followed by dropping 5 μL of the mercaptohexanol (MCH) solution (1 mM), incubating for 2 h, washing with water and drying at 4 °C in order. Before use, the prepared sensor was stored at 4 °C.

All electrochemical measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out on a CHI660D electrochemical workstation. The classical three-electrode system was employed for the electrochemical measurement. the gold modified by H1 and H3, Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum wire were used as the working electrode, the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The EIS measurements were measured at the frequency range 100 KHz and 1 Hz with the potential amplitude of ±5 mV. The DPV parameters were set to the scan rate of 4 mV s−1, 50 mV pulse amplitude, 20 ms pulse width and −0.2 V initial potential.

Before the detection, the AP aqueous solution (10 μM) was mixed with an equal volume of AS solution (10 μM) and then incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to produce AP/AS duplex DNA. For the detection, 10 μL of the AP/AS duplex DNA solution was mixed with 40 μL of α-amanitin standard solution with different concentrations and then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. The resultant mixed solution (20 μL) was mixed with 10 μL of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI solution and 40 μL of the TE buffer. Then, its 5 μL was dropped to the sensor surface. The sensor was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, washed with water and used for differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurement in the PBS of pH 7.0 in the potential range from −0.3 V to −0.1 V.

Scheme for the synthesis of AGB-GQD@Au was presented in Fig. 1 . Firstly, AGB-GQD is prepared by pyrolysis of mixture of citric acid, aspartic acid, glycine acid and boric acid. Here, citric acid acts as carbon source for making graphene sheet [29]. Aspartic acid and glycine acid act as functional reagents for introducing functional groups. Because carboxyl group includes one large π-electron conjugate system, the introduction of aspartic acid and glycine acid widens electron migration range in graphene sheet. This reduces the electron density in functional groups and increases electron density in graphene sheet. This leads to an increased energy level of AGB-GQD. Boric acid acts as dopant to introduce boron atoms into carbon lattice. Because of strong electron-withdrawing ability, the doped boron atoms strongly attract electrons from peripheral functional groups to graphene sheet, enhancing the electron mobility between graphene sheet and peripheral functional groups. This results in a reduced energy level. To confirm the suggestion, four GQDs, including classical GQD (GQD), aspartic acid-functionalized GQD (A-GQD), aspartic acid and glycine acid-co-functionalized GQD (AG-GQD) and AGB-GQD, were synthesized by the same procedure. Their UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured and then their photoelectron energy levels were calculated by basing on the slopes of curves in Fig. S1. The energy levels are 2.68 eV for GQD, 2.83 eV for A-GQD, 2.81 eV for AG-GQD and 2.42 eV for AGB-GQD. These data demonstrate that the introduction of aspartic acid and glycine acid increases the energy level and boron atom reduces the energy level.

The as-prepared AGB-GQD was used for the synthesis of AGB-GQD@Au. As AGB-GQD has strong reduction ability, the synthesis can complete within 1 min at 100 °C. When HAuCl4 was added to the AGB-GQD solution, the color of the reaction system rapidly turned black from red (shown in Fig. S2. Here, Au3+ was reduced to Au0 and finally formed gold nanocrystals. Meanwhile, some AGB-GQDs were bounded on the surface of gold nanocrystals by N–Au and O–Au. The immobilization of AGB-GQD results in improvement of water-solubility of gold nanocrystals and formation of Schottky heterojunctions.

Fig. 2 presents the SEM image, TEM image, XRD pattern and FTIR spectrum of AGB-GQD@Au. The SEM and TEM images show AGB-GQD@Au has sphere-like nanostructure with an average particle size is 12.5 ± 0.22 nm (shown in Fig. S3). The XRD pattern includes five diffraction peaks at 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.6°, 77.5° and 81.7°, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) facets of Au nanocrystal. The result demonstrates the reduction of HAuCl4 with AGB-GQD can form Au nanocrystals. Due to a poor crystallinity of AGB-GQD, the pattern doesn't appear the characteristic diffraction peak at 26° of common graphene. The FTIR spectrum contains several IR absorption bands and absorption peaks. The absorption band between 3600 and 3000 cm−1 is attributed to symmetric vibration of the N–H and O–H bonds. The peak at 2933 cm−1 is caused by stretching vibration of the C–H bond. The peak at 1710 cm−1 is the IR absorption of stretching vibration of the CO bond. AGB-GQD contains rich carboxyl groups, thus the peak at 1710 cm−1 has the maximum absorption intensity on the IR spectrum. The peak at 1673 cm−1 can be assigned to the IR absorption caused by bending vibration of the N–H bond plane and asymmetric stretching vibration of the C–O bond. The peak at 1400 cm−1 is attributed to the IR absorption of the -N-CO bond. The peaks at 1179 and 1041 cm−1 are caused by the out-of-plane bending vibration of O–H.

The XPS spectra of AGB-GQD@Au are presented in Fig. 3 . The total XPS spectrum includes five main peaks at 86, 190, 284, 399, and 532 eV, corresponding to the Au 4f, B 1s, C1s, N1s and O1s. The result verifies that AGB-GQD@Au is composed of Au, B, C, N and O elements. Au element only comes from Au nanocrystal, and B, C, N and O elements come from AGB-GQD. High-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum can be divided into five peaks at 283.8, 284.2, 285.4, 287.4 and 288.8 eV. These peaks correspond to the C–B, CC, C–N, CO and o-CO bonds, verifying the existence of OH, –NH2, and –COOH groups. High-resolution B 1s XPS spectrum includes three peaks at 189.6190.0 and 190.5 eV, corresponding to the B–N, B–O and B–C bonds. The existence of B–N and B–O demonstrates that some boron atoms are at the edge of graphene sheets. High-resolution Au4f XPS spectrum contains four peaks at 81.7, 82.1, 85.2 and 85.4 eV. These peaks are corresponded to the Au04f7/2, Au+14f5/2, Au04f5/2 and Au+14f7/2, indicating the existence of Au0 atoms and Au + ions. As the peak area of Au04f7/2 and Au04f5/2 is much bigger than that of Au+14f7/2 and Au+14f5/2, the Au0 is the main specie of Au element in AGB-GQD@Au. Strong electron-withdrawing effect of boron atoms in carbon lattice weakens the Au–Au bond, thus the binding energy of Au4f is slightly smaller than that in the reported GQD/Au composite [30]. High-resolution N1s XPS spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 399.4, 400.9 and 401.8 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N, graphite N and oxide N. The pyrrolic N and graphitic N come from doping N atoms in carbon lattice and edge of graphene sheet at high temperature, while the oxide N comes from the -N-CO and NHCO groups. High-resolution O1s XPS spectrum contains two peaks at 528.9 and 530.7 eV. These peaks can be assigned to the O–H and B–O bonds, verifying the existence of –OH and –COOH groups.

To confirm the formation of Schottky heterojunction, Mott-Schottky plots of AGB-GQD and AGB-GQD@Au were measured and the results were shown in Fig. S4. The line sections of Mott-Schottky plots of AGB-GQD and AGB-GQD@Au offer a negative slope, verifying that AGB-GQD and AGB-GQD@Au are p-type semiconductors. Further, the flat band potential (Vb) was obtained by extrapolating the Mott-Schottky plot. The Vb values are −1.0 V for AGB-GQD and −1.09 V for AGB-GQD@Au, demonstrating that the combination of AGB-GQD with Au nanocrystal brings a more negative Vb shift. This increases the electron concentration and electrical conductivity and leads to improved catalytic activity.

Fig. 4 presents the scheme for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin. Firstly, the sensor was prepared by immobilizing pre-treated H1 on the gold electrode (GE) surface via Au–S bond and subsequently blocking remaining active sites with MCH. The as-prepared sensor was used for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin coupled with the target-induced DNA cycle. Before the detection, aptamer (AP) hybridizes with auxiliary strand DNA (AS) to form stable AP/AS duplex DNA. In the presence of α-amanitin, target specifically binds with AP in the duplex DNA to release one free AS. The released AS triggers the DNA cycle and brings one H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe to the sensor surface. The redox of THI molecules in the probe produces a sensitive electrochemical response. Through the DNA cycle, one target molecule can transfer many H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes to the sensor surface, producing a significant detection signal amplification. However, the DNA cycle cannot be triggered in the absence of α-amanitin. Since no H2-AGB-GQD@Au probes were brought to the sensor surface during the incubation, the sensor only provided a weak background signal. For the above reasons, the proposed sensing platform can be applied to electrochemical detection of α-amanitin.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) behaviors of different electrodes were studied in a PBS of pH 7.4 containing 5.0 mM of K4[Fe(CN)6] to examine the feasibility of the proposed sensing platform for detection of α-amanitin. Fig. 5 shows all electrodes offer a pair of redox peaks on the CV curves, because of the oxidation and reduction of Fe(CN)6 4− on the electrode surface. The H1/GE has a lower CV current compared with bare GE. The immobilization of H1 partly blocks the electron transfer channels between the electrode and the electrolyte. This leads to a reduced CV current with an increased charge transfer impedance (Rct) (shown in Table S1). Similar to H1, the introduction of MCH further reduces the CV current with an increased Rct value. In the absence of α-amanitin, the incubation of MCH/H1/RE in the mixed AP/AS duplex DNA solution with H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes only brings a weak change in the CV current and Rct value. In the absence of α-amanitin, the DNA cycle cannot be triggered. As no H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes were transferred to the sensor surface during the incubation, the sensor remains relatively stable in CV current and Rct response. In the presence of 4 × 10−12 M of α-amanitin, the incubation produces a significantly increased CV current. The result confirms that the DNA cycle can be triggered in the presence of α-amanitin. By the DNA cycle, few α-amanitin molecules can bring many H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes to the sensor surface. The redox of THI molecules in these H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes on the electrode surface causes an increased CV current and a reduced Rct value. The above results demonstrate that the proposed sensing platform has high feasibility for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin.

The DPV behaviors of proposed sensing platform were studied in the PBS of pH 7 in the absence and the presence of 4 × 10−12 M α-amanitin. Fig. 6 shows that the DPV curve is almost a straight line in the absence of α-amanitin and no DPV peak can observe in the potential range from −0.3 V to −0.15 V. This is because no redox probes can be brought to the sensor surface in the absence of α-amanitin during the incubation. As a result, no peaks caused by the redox of THI molecules appear on the DPV curve. However, the introduction of α-amanitin brings a significantly increased DPV peak current. Such a high DPV response is attributed to the gold-graphene quantum dot nanohybrid and DNA cycle dual signal amplification. By the DNA cycle, one target molecule brings many redox probes to the sensor surface. Their redox on the sensor surface produces a significant signal amplification. In addition, AGB-GQD@Au catalyzes the redox of THI molecules. The catalysis accelerates electrode reactions and results in enhanced detection signals. This further improves the sensitivity for detection of α-amanitin.

To examine the role of AGB-GQD, AGB-GQD@Au and Au nanoparticles (Au) were used as sensing materials for DPV detection of 4 × 10−12 M α-amanitin by the same sensing platform, respectively. Fig. S5A shows the use of AGB-GQD@Au brings a better DPV current response towards α-amanitin compared with Au. This is due to the introduction of AGB-GQD. As AGB-GQD is one p-type semiconductor, the combination of AGB-GQD and Au nanocrystal creates the Schottky heterojunction at the interface. The formation of Schottky heterojunction will improve the electron transfer between AGB-GQD and Au nanoparticles and leads to an improved catalytic activity, which is confirmed by the result of the SERS test in Fig. S5B. Such an improved catalytic activity accelerates the redox of THI molecules and results in a more sensitive DPV current response.

Effects of the incubation time and volume of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution on the DPV peak currwere was studied in a PBS of pH 7.4 in the presence of 1 × 10−12 M α-amanitin. Fig. S6A indicates the change in DPV peak current (ΔIp) increases with the increase of the incubation time if the incubation time is less than 60 min. This is because the number of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes on the sensor surface increases with increasing incubation time. The redox of more THI probes on the sensor surface results in an increased ΔIp value. However, the ΔIp value tends to be stable if the incubation time is more than 60 min. This is because the capture of the sensor towards H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes and their leave from the sensor surface reaches one thermodynamic equilibrium. In the current state, the number of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes on the sensor surface no longer increases with the incubation time. Therefore, the ΔIp value doesn't bring a significant change with increasing incubation time. To obtain high sensitivity and save time, the incubation time of 60 min was chosen for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin.

Fig. S6B presents the relationship curve of ΔIp value with the volume of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution. It can be seen from Fig. S6B If the volume is less than 10 μL, the Ip value will increase with an increasing volume of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution. This is because in a low probe concentration the number of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes is less than that of the redox probes that can be immobilized on the electrode surface by the DNA cycle. Thus, the number of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes immobilized on the electrode surface only depends on the volume of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution. As a result, the ΔIp value increase with the volume increase of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution due to the redox of more probes. However, the ΔIp value reaches the maximum value and then tends to be stable if the volume of H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution is more than 10 μL. This is because the number of probes can meet the needs of the DNA cycle during the incubation. Continuing to increase the volume of the H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution will not significantly increase the number of redox probes immobilized on the electrode surface by the DNA cycle. Therefore, the ΔIp value tends to be stable. To obtain high sensitivity and save the probes, the H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probe solution of 10 μL was used for the electrochemical detection of α-amanitin.

To test the practicability of proposed sensing platform for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin, the sensor was incubated in an α-amanitin solution with different concentrations for 60 min at 37 °C and then their DPV curves were measured in the PBS of pH 7. Fig. 7 A shows the DPV currents will rapidly increase with the increase of α-amanitin concentration. This is because all α-amanitin molecules in the detection system are involved in the hybridization of AP in AP/AS duplex DNA. A higher concentration of α-amanitin leads to the release of more AS molecules. The increase of free AS molecules accelerates the DNA cycle rate. As a result, more H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes were brought to the sensor surface during the incubation time of 60 min. This results in an increased DPV peak current. Further, Fig. 7B shows one relationship curve of DPV peak current with logarithm value of the α-amanitin concentrations. There is a good linear relationship between the DPV peak current (Ip) and the logarithm of α-amanitin concentration in the range from 4 to 4 × 105 fM. The corresponding linear regression equation is: the Ip (nA) = 50.27 × Lg[Cα-amanitin, fM] + 339.7, with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99199. The limit of detection (LOD) was measured and calculated by basing on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3). This indicates the proposed method has better sensitivity compared with other methods Table S2.

Fifty sensors were prepared and used for electrochemical detection of 1 × 10−11 M α-amanitin by the same procedure and materials. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of DPV peak current responses among these sensors is 2.4%. The same sensor was used detection of 1 × 10−11 M α-amanitin twenty times. The RSD value of twenty times detections is 1.9%. The as-prepared sensor was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C, taken out every other week and used for detection of 1 × 10−11 M α-amanitin. The result shows that the four-week storage only produces a change of 2.4% in the DPV peak current. The above results demonstrate that the proposed sensor has good reproducibility, repeatability and storage stability for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin.

To test the anti-interference of proposed method, the effect of some inorganic ionic and organic small molecule compounds that often exist in blood, and toxins that may appear in mushrooms on the DPV peak current response were investigated. The result shows 100 μg mL−1 of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, Cl−, SO4 2−, ascorbic acid, glucose and citric acid cause a change (ΔIp) of less than 5% in the DPV peak current (ΔIp). Among α-amanitin, β-amanitin(β-AMA), γ-amanitin(γ-AMA), chlorpyrifos (CHL), carbendazim(CAR), acetamiprid (ACE), glutathione (GSH), tryptophan (Try) and human serum protein (HSA), only α-amanitin can produce a sensitive DPV peak current response (shown in Fig. S7). The above results verify that the proposed method has good selectivity for detection of α-amanitin. This is because the other toxins cannot trigger the DNA cycle. Because no H2-AGB-GQD@Au-THI probes were brought to the sensor surface during the incubation, the sensor offers a weak DPV peak current that is close to the blank test.

The blood samples were obtained from healthy volunteers from Jiangnan University. The blood sample of 0.5 mL was mixed with 0.1 mL of α-amanitin standard solution with a known concentration. Then, it was orderly treated by addition of 1 mL of 1% (v/v) acetic acid-acetonitrile solution, ultrasonic treatment for 2 min and centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The collected supernatant was transferred to another centrifuge tube. After adding 5 mL of dichloromethane, the tube was inverted several times. Followed by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5min and addition of 0.5 mL of 2% (v/v) acetic acid/water solution into the upper aqueous phase. The upper water phase was collected and used for electrochemical detection of α-amanitin. Table 1 shows that the recoveries are in the range of 99.4–101.8%, indicating good precision and accuracy.

The study has demonstrated design and synthesis of gold-aspartic acid, glycine acid-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot nanohybrid. The nanohybrid offers a small particle size of gold nanocrystal and Schottky heterojunction at the interface. The structural characteristic makes nanohybrid has good catalytic activity, electron conductivity and hydrophilicity. The electrochemical sensor based on gold-graphene quantum dot nanohybrid and DNA cycle dual signal amplification exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability for detection of α-amanitin. The study also provides an approach for construction of metal-graphene nanohybrid with an ideal catalytic activity in sensing and catalysis.
Ruiyi Li: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Wang Jin: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Li Nana: Investigation; Xu Dan: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910203) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.
